description.text:
### Description

If I rename a document using `Ctrl-R` and attempt to export it immediately afterward, nothing happens. The problem goes away if I click over to another tab and return to the original document, after which export works again.

### Reproducing

Create and save a markdown file and add some content to it. Then rename the file using the `Ctrl-R` keyboard shortcut. Attempt to export the file.

For what it's worth, I'm using `Microsoft Word (docx)` and `Select Directory` as my export presets.

### Zettlr version

3.4.1-nightly from 2025-01-13

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [x] Linux

### Architecture

- [x] Intel 64bit
- [ ] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon)

### Operating System Version

Manjaro (Rolling)

### Additional Information

I'm using the appimage.

summary: Prioritize Previously Used Options in the Export Menu

description.text:
### Description

I love the variety of formats that Zettlr can export in and the ability to create custom export templates. At the same time, amid the long list of options there are ones that I've never used and likely will never have occasion to use. I think the menu of export options might be easier to navigate if the previously used options were sorted to the top.

### Proposed Changes

Currently, when the user selects the `Format` dropdown menu from the export pop-up, they get a long list of options. The sort order for this list is unclear. It appears to be partially alphabetical, but some options are not in alphabetical order. As a result, the user has to hunt through the list carefully to find the option they want:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c411b598-6239-46f2-92f5-abe575976486)

This state of affairs could be improved by sorting the list alphabetically. Additionally, though, I think usability would be greatly improved by prioritizing the options the user has made use of previously. An analogous example comes from the font selection menu used in most word processors. For example, here's how LibreOffice sorts fonts — with recently used options up top, separated by a horizontal rule from the remaining fonts, which appear in alphabetical order:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d5b6ad97-ea1e-4677-b463-e1c690d9a894)

Google Docs uses a similar convention:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d6627a5e-4b01-459d-a757-094dbb411621)

In my envisioned scenario, Zettlr's export menu would similarly keep recently used options up top and — beneath a horizontal rule — place the rest of the options in alphabetical order. For example, if I've used the `XeLaTeX PDF`, `Simple PDF`, and `Microsoft Word` options, but haven't touched the others (or haven't used them recently) the menu might go something like this:

```
XeLaTeX PDF (pdf)
Simple PDF (pdf)
Microsoft Word (docx)
---------------------
HTML (html)
LaTeX to Markdown (markdown)
LaTeX (latex)
…
```

There are a few remaining points to consider:

1. _How many recent options should be promoted to the top?_ I'd suggest somewhere between three and five. Alternatively, all formats that have been used could be promoted, leaving those that have never been touched by the user in the lower list.
2. _When should an option be considered "used?"_ I'd suggest Zettlr consider an export option as having been used only when the user has actually run the export process with it. In other words, merely selecting a format option, but not submitting the export form, should not be enough to promote it.
3. _When should items be demoted back to the lower list?_ This could be by time (e.g., not used in a month) or by order of selection (e.g., the three most recently used formats, regardless of when they were chosen). The latter probably makes the most sense.
4. _Should the list be document-specific?_ I think this would be great, since the user may use one set of export formats for journal articles and another for departmental memos. At the same time, I think a global "recently used" sort order would still be preferable to the status quo.

### Caveats

None that I can think of.

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Nightly

### Your Platform

- [ ] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Operating System Version

Manjaro (Rolling)

### Additional Information

_No response_

summary: Batch Export Files in a Workspace

description.text:
### Checklist

- [X] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] Zettlr's current features cannot be used to approximate this behaviour (otherwise, propose an enhancement instead.)
- [ ] I have searched the documentation to make sure I did not overlook it.
- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

When working with an adviser or developmental editor, there may be times when a user would like to export all the notes in a workspace folder in a shareable format, like `.docx` or `.pdf`. While it's currently possible to set a workspace folder to project mode and export its contents as a combined document, there is currently no straightforward way to export all the notes in a workspace folder as individual documents. This would be a handy feature to have and would be useful both for sharing a collection of notes and for data portability.

### Example Workflow

My suggested implementation is this:

1. The user would right-click the target workspace folder in the file manager and select a new option — e.g., `Batch export markdown files` — from the resulting context menu.
2. A pop-up dialogue would appear, just as it currently does when you select `Properties` from the same context menu. However, the pop-up dialogue for the `Batch export` option would be identical to the dialogue that currently appears when the user exports an individual file:

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5e19fdae-0a1e-4dc0-8f70-2612e8024b78)

3. The user would select their preferred export options (e.g., `Format` and either `Current directory` or `Select directory` — `Temporary directory` would likely be stricken) and click the `Export` button to batch-export all the files in the workspace.
4. Where an individual file export opens the exported file in an external application, batch export could instead open the containing folder for the exported documents in the Finder/File Explorer.

### Caveats

Not applicable.

### Do you wish to attempt implementing this yourself?

No

### Platforms

- [X] Windows
- [X] macOS
- [X] Linux

### Additional Information

I'll just mention that there are currently two workarounds that users can use to batch-export notes. One is to use `pandoc` on the command line or another external app/script to do the exporting. The other is to create a script to iterate through and export all the files in a project folder and add it to Zettlr using the `Custom Export Commands` feature. This feature suggestion would simply save users the additional work of implementing such workarounds, while making the functionality accessible to folks without the technical skills to implement them.

summary: Horizontal rules rendering in the editor

description.text:
### Checklist

- [X] Zettlr does not possess this feature (if it does, but the feature doesn't work out for you, propose an enhancement instead.)

- [ ] I have searched both open and closed issues to see if someone has already proposed this.

### Feature Proposal

Is it possible to render the horizontal rules directly in the editor, the same way they render in the documents exported.

### Example Workflow

Typing "***" or "---" render the horizontal rule in the editor, instead of letting only the characters as they are typed.

### Caveats

I don't think there will be any caveats

summary: Horizontal rule not rendering when exporting to simple pdf, works when exporting to .docx and .odt

description.text:
### Description

In a document, I typed "---" and "***" but none of them are making a horizontal rule when exporting as simple PDF. 

### Reproducing

Try to type --- or *** in a document, and then export to Simple PDF.

### Zettlr version

3.4.1

### Installation Method

From the Website or GitHub

### Installation Method (Other)

_No response_

### Your Platform

- [X] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Architecture

- [X] Intel 64bit
- [ ] ARM 64bit (includes Apple Silicon)

### Operating System Version

Windows 11

### Additional Information

_No response_

## Description
<!-- Below, please describe what the PR does in one or two short sentences. -->

This PR fixes the FSAL Caching procedure, which only happened partially before. This was due to the fact that (a) the FSAL provider has been booted very late in the chain, and (b) the asynchronous unlinking was happening while the rest of the party was continuing to boot.

Specifically, this led to the following chain of events:

* The user passes the `--clear-cache` command to the application (usually using the "Help" menu and restarting) or updates to a new version
* The FSAL begins its boot process
* The FSAL detects that it should clear its cache, and calls the clear method of the Cache
* The Cache immediately clears its in-memory cache and lists all cache files for deletion
* It starts calling unlinks on the various files, but using `fs.unlink`-callbacks and collects them in promises. However, it does not await the promises, and instead returns immediately.
* The FSAL boot process concludes.
* Other providers that need to access files start to boot
* Some cache files are deleted at this point
* Other cache files are being read because they are requested
* Those files are then being deleted
* (…and so on)

You see where the issue lies: Before the cache could be cleared in its entirety, other providers will request files, causing not-yet-deleted cache files to be read, meaning that parts of the cache are effectively not cleared.

## Changes
<!-- What changes did you make? Please explicitly state any breaking API changes so that nobody is confused why other components suddenly stop working -->

1. Fix the boot order. I am not 100% sure if this was completely necessary, but it made a lot of sense. The new boot order seems to work appropriately.
2. Promisify the cache clearing procedure to ensure that, once the FSAL boot function returns, the cache is guaranteed to be cleared.

## Additional information
<!-- If there is anything else that might be of interest, please provide it here -->

* Fixes #5552

@kyaso It would be great to have someone else with a different setup double check this, but I assume it should just work. Nevertheless, I wanted to start pushing *every* fix of mine as a PR rather than messing with the develop branch directly.

<!-- Please provide any testing system -->
Tested on: macOS Sequoia 15.2

summary: Allow the table of contents to begin numbering at heading level h2.

description.text:
### Description

Hi, for us folks that are using first level heading h1 as a title of the file (and no other first level headings ever after), I would like to see the numbering in the table of content to start at the second level heading h2. Currently, with mentioned approach, all table items are prefixed with the never-changing "1.", which makes deeper headings' numbers unnecessarily longer and thus less readable.

### Proposed Changes

I would like a checkbox somewhere in the preferences setup, that will switch numbering starting point between h1 and h2.

### Caveats

Being that processed, it might get further extended to any heading depth choice or even no numbering at all (indentation only).

### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself?

No

### Zettlr Version

Stable (most recent version)

### Your Platform

- [X] Windows
- [ ] macOS
- [ ] Linux

### Operating System Version

Windows 11

### Additional Information

_No response_

### Description I spend a lot of time switching between Zettlr and GitHub. For example, I'll draft a document in Zettlr and then open a PR with that document in GitHub. I'd love to see a theme that looks roughly like GitHub so that what I see in Zettlr more closely matches what I get in GitHub. This theme would underline links so that folks with color-blindness can easily distinguish links from regular text. ### Proposed Changes I'd like to see a new theme that looks like the "light default" and "dark default" themes in GitHub. I've [written some custom CSS](https://gist.github.com/rdebeasi/adc961c2d20d7163d7cf7fe19fa5eb1e) to make Zettlr's Berlin theme look a bit more like the GitHub's "light default" theme. ### Caveats - If Zettlr had a theme manager (#2861), this feature might make sense in a custom theme rather than in Zettlr itself. - GitHub's default theme may change over time, and this theme might have to be updated to change with it. - The gist linked above doesn't implement dark mode. - The gist above styles the highlight area to look like the macOS default highlight, which might look odd on other platforms, or if a different accent color is selected on macOS. ### Do you Wish to Attempt Implementing this Yourself? Yes ### Zettlr Version Stable (most recent version) ### Your Platform - [X] macOS ### Operating System Version macOS 15.2 ### Additional Information
